title: Hans Aarsleff and the uses of error
gist: Through the career of linguist-historian Hans Aarsleff, the essay argues that 'error' deserves a central place in intellectual history - bad ideas like Horne Tooke's etymology or Chomsky's 'Cartesian Linguistics' can be as influential as true ones, and historians should judge, not merely steelman, past arguments.

insights.claim: Aarsleff's central claim is that in the history of ideas 'error may be as influential as truth', so influential errors deserve study rather than dismissal.
insights.so_what: A reusable lens: track why wrong ideas win and persist, not just which ideas were correct.
insights.quote: Error may be as influential as truth
insights.speaker: Samuel Rubinstein

insights.claim: Horne Tooke's etymology-based 'pseudoscience' in The Diversions of Purley (1786) dominated English linguistics for decades despite the superior comparative philology of William Jones developing on the continent.
insights.so_what: Case study in an inferior theory beating a superior one through cultural entrenchment.
insights.speaker: Samuel Rubinstein

insights.claim: Aarsleff argued intellectual historians should sit in judgment of past ideas rather than merely 'steelman' them, warning that suspending all judgement is a first step toward arbitrary relativism.
insights.so_what: A methodological stance against pure relativism in the history of ideas.
insights.speaker: Samuel Rubinstein

insights.claim: In 1970 Aarsleff accused Noam Chomsky of 'plain ignorance', arguing his 'Cartesian Linguistics' (1966) was wrong on every substantive point and had 'significantly set back' the history of linguistics.
insights.so_what: Notable scholarly takedown of a canonical figure's self-constructed intellectual pedigree.
insights.speaker: Samuel Rubinstein

insights.claim: The essay suggests dominant theories can succeed by 'outbullying' opponents - a charge levelled at Horne Tooke and implicitly at Chomsky's grip over mid-20th-century linguistics.
insights.so_what: Reputation and rhetorical force, not correctness, can determine which ideas prevail.
insights.speaker: Samuel Rubinstein

insights.claim: Rigorous comparative philology was developed by Germans (Schlegel, Bopp, the Brothers Grimm) and Danes (Rask, Grundtvig) while England lagged badly in the 1820s-30s.
insights.so_what: Illustrates cross-national divergence in the pace of intellectual progress.
insights.speaker: Samuel Rubinstein
